semantic_journey: Arabic Slav ethnonym is itself borrowed FROM Greek. Arabic did not originate this ethnonym. Both words name the same people; Arabic received the term from Greek. Shared ethnonym with Arabic receiving from Greek, not the reverse.
key_insight: Arabic Slav ethnonym borrowed FROM Greek - ethnonym traveled Greek-to-Arabic, not the reverse.

semantic_journey: Arabic farkh = chick, nestling, young bird (Semitic root for young of a bird). Greek pharkes = young birds, nestlings, chicks (Hesychius: neossoi = nestlings). The semantic match is direct and precise: both mean exactly young birds/nestlings. The Semitic root f-r-kh for young bird is preserved in Greek. Genuine direct preservation - same bird-life vocabulary with no drift.
key_insight: Arabic farkh (nestling/chick) directly matches Greek pharkes (nestlings) - genuine direct preservation of bird-life vocabulary.

semantic_journey: Arabic naft = naphtha, petroleum, the naturally occurring flammable liquid. Greek naftha = naphtha. Semitic root n-f-t for the substance was transmitted to Greek. This is a documented Semitic loanword into Greek - the flammable earth-substance and its Semitic name were transmitted together. Material culture transmission.
key_insight: Semitic naphtha term (n-f-t) entered Greek - genuine material culture cognate for petroleum.

semantic_journey: Arabic farkhash = variant root related to young bird/chick (similar to farkh in GRC_0025). Greek pharkes = young birds/nestlings. Same as GRC_0025 - direct preservation of Semitic bird-life vocabulary. The root variant still centers on young bird.
key_insight: Root variant of GRC_0025 - Arabic young-bird concept preserved in Greek pharkes, medium confidence due to root variant uncertainty.

semantic_journey: Arabic thunderbolt/being struck senseless (physical impact). Greek means to be faint-hearted, to be of little spirit/courage (psychological state). Physical impact by lightning and inner psychological weakness are distinct concepts. The chain thunderbolt -> struck senseless -> faint -> faint-hearted requires 3+ steps. No clear path.
key_insight: Arabic thunderbolt/being struck and Greek faint-heartedness are distinct semantic domains - 3+ step stretch.

semantic_journey: Arabic and Greek are proper names from different cultures and regions (Thracian prince vs Arabic name form). Coincidental phonetic resemblance. Excluded as proper names.
key_insight: Two unrelated proper names (Thracian prince vs Arabic name) - accidental phonetic resemblance.

semantic_journey: Arabic henna = henna (Lawsonia inermis, a dye plant). Greek lakhamon = garden vegetable/edible herb. Henna is a dye plant not a food herb; Greek specifically means edible garden greens. These are different categories of cultivated plants. The semantic distance (dye plant vs food vegetable) is too large. No clear semantic path.
key_insight: Arabic henna (dye plant) and Greek garden vegetable (lakhamon) are different plant categories - not a real match.

semantic_journey: Arabic and Greek appear to be proper place names or transmitted name forms. Without clear documentation of a productive Arabic root driving the connection, this is at best a proper name coincidence. Classified as proper name.
key_insight: Possible place-name continuity but insufficient evidence for a productive root cognate - classified as proper name.
